turns.content: I'm working through some exercises in the Rust Programming Book and I thought I could just use a standard library counter for testing metrics. Does the std::collections::HashMap actually support the Record trait so I can avoid writing custom logic?

That's not quite right; the standard library HashMap does not implement the Recorder trait, which means you cannot simply drop it into your metrics pipeline without extra work. Think of it like trying to put a square peg in a round hole—the tool isn't shaped correctly for the job even if it looks similar on the surface. You'll need to build something that actually satisfies the trait requirements.
Okay, so I should probably write my own struct and implement Recorder myself? But how do I handle sending those metrics to multiple backends like Prometheus or OpenTelemetry without duplicating code?

You can use the in-memory recorder from crates.io as a central store, then have your custom structs write to each backend individually. Imagine having one master mailbox where all letters arrive, and then you send those same letters to different post offices using separate drivers; that way the data lives once but reaches everywhere it needs.
That analogy makes sense now. So if I'm building a custom struct for tracking latency, do I need to manually parse every metric field or can I rely on some automatic serialization?

For most practical cases you should implement manual parsing of the fields yourself rather than relying on auto-serialization. The trait requires explicit control over how values are formatted, so letting serde_json or similar libraries handle every detail often breaks compatibility with the recorder interface.
